6: The fall of Samaria (722 BCE) to the Assyrian Empire (under Sargon II, 2 Kings 17:6) ended the northern kingdom of Israel after 210 years and 19 kings (every one of whom 'did evil in the sight of YHWH'). The author of Kings provides the theological explanation in 2 Kings 17:7-23: because Israel sinned against YHWH, walked in the customs of the nations, built high places, served Baal — YHWH removed them out of his sight. The Assyrian exile of the northern tribes ('the ten lost tribes') was permanent: they were never restored as a distinct entity. The northern exile is the first fulfillment of the Deuteronomic covenant curses (Deuteronomy 28:36, 64); the Babylonian exile of Judah (586 BCE) is the second. Both are explained as covenant consequences, not historical accidents.
1: Hoshea son of Elah begins his reign in Samaria in the 12th year of Ahaz (c. 732 BCE), reigning nine years (c. 732–723 BCE). He is the last king of the northern kingdom — 19 kings from Jeroboam I to Hoshea, none of whom turned from the sins of Jeroboam. Hoshea's name (hôšēaʿ — salvation, deliverance) is the same root as Joshua and Jesus; the final northern king bears the name of salvation but delivers his kingdom into captivity. He was installed by Tiglath-pileser III after killing Pekah (2 Kings 15:30) and was from the beginning an Assyrian vassal.
2: The evaluative formula for Hoshea is uniquely qualified: 'He did what was evil in the sight of the LORD, though not to the degree of the kings of Israel before him' (rāʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH raq lōʾ ḵᵉmalkê Yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer hāyû lᵉpānāyw). This is the only northern king to receive a qualified negative evaluation. The basis of the qualification is unclear — possibly Hoshea permitted more direct YHWH worship, possibly the Deuteronomist is acknowledging that even within the unbroken negative trajectory there were degrees of apostasy. The qualification does not save Hoshea or the kingdom; the trajectory toward exile is unaffected by relative improvement.
3: Shalmaneser V (ruled 727–722 BCE) — son and successor of Tiglath-pileser III — marches against Hoshea. Hoshea 'became his vassal and paid him tribute' (wayhî lô ʿeḇed wayyāšeḇ lô minḥāh); the minḥāh (tribute, literally gift-offering) is the same word used for the sacrificial grain offering, suggesting the political tribute-language and the cultic offering-language share a vocabulary of submission. Hoshea's vassalage is initially compliant, continuing the tribute-payment pattern established by Menahem.
4: Hoshea's conspiracy with 'So king of Egypt' (śōʾ melek miṣrāyim) and withholding of annual tribute triggers Shalmaneser's response. 'So' has been variously identified as Osorkon IV (the last pharaoh of the 22nd dynasty, ruling in Tanis), Tefnakht of Sais (a Delta king), or Sibe the Egyptian commander mentioned in Sargon II's annals. Egypt was entering a period of political fragmentation (the Third Intermediate Period) and was not capable of providing effective military assistance; the appeal to Egypt was strategically futile. Hoshea's arrest and imprisonment before the full siege indicates Shalmaneser may have intercepted the diplomatic correspondence, exposing the conspiracy before Hoshea could fully mobilize against Assyria.
5: The three-year siege of Samaria (c. 725–722 BCE) is one of the longer sieges recorded in the biblical narrative. Samaria, built on a naturally defensible hill (purchased by Omri, 1 Kings 16:24), was difficult to take by direct assault; Assyrian siege techniques (ramps, battering rams, siege towers) required time against a well-supplied hilltop city. The three-year resistance indicates significant food and water reserves in the city. Shalmaneser V died during or shortly after the siege; his successor Sargon II claims the actual capture in his own annals (ANET 284-285: 'I besieged and conquered Samaria, led away as booty 27,290 inhabitants of it').
7: The Deuteronomist begins the longest theological explanation in Kings (vv7-23), unparalleled in scope in the entire narrative. The explanation opens with the covenantal framework: 'This happened because the Israelites had sinned against the LORD their God, who had brought them out of Egypt' (wayhî kî ḥāṭᵉʾû bᵉnê-Yiśrāʾēl laYHWH ʾelōhêhem hammaʿᵃleh ʾōtām mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim). The Exodus is the defining covenant act; Israel's election and obligation is rooted in YHWH's redemptive initiative. Sin is framed as covenant violation — not merely moral failure but the betrayal of a specific relationship established at Sinai. Worshipping other gods is the fundamental breach (v7b: wayyirᵉʾû ʾelōhîm ʾăḥērîm).
8: The charge that Israel 'followed the practices of the nations the LORD had driven out before them' (wayyēlᵉḵû bᵉḥuqqôt haggôyim ʾăšer hôrîš YHWH mippᵉnê bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl) is the Deuteronomistic irony: Israel was to be the replacement population for the nations; instead, Israel adopted the nations' practices and is now itself replaced by imported nations (v24). The divine-expulsion logic that justified the Conquest (Deuteronomy 9:4-5 — the nations were driven out for their wickedness, not Israel's righteousness) now applies to Israel: YHWH expels covenant-breakers regardless of their ethnic identity.
9: Israel 'secretly did things against the LORD their God that were not right' (wayhappāsēq bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl dᵉḇārîm ʾăšer lōʾ ḵēn ʿal YHWH ʾelōhêhem) — the word hippāsēq (Piel of a root meaning to cover, conceal) indicates the covert nature of the apostasy. The installation of high places 'from the smallest watchtower to the largest fortified city' (mimmigdal nōṣᵉrîm ʿaḏ ʿîr miḇṣār) describes comprehensive geographic coverage: every inhabited site, from the most remote watchpost to the most fortified urban center, had unauthorized cultic installations. The scale of the apostasy matches the scale of the judgment.
10: The sacred pillars (maṣṣēḇôt) and Asherah poles (ʾăšērîm) are the two most frequently condemned cult installations in the Deuteronomistic literature. Maṣṣēḇôt were standing stones used as markers of divine presence, originally acceptable in the patriarchal period (Genesis 28:18) but condemned in Deuteronomy 12:3 and 2 Kings 16:22. ʾĂšērîm were wooden poles or trees representing Asherah, the Canaanite mother-goddess and consort of El; archaeological evidence from Kuntillet Ajrud (c. 800 BCE) includes inscriptions referencing 'YHWH of Samaria and his Asherah,' indicating that Asherah veneration was integrated into some forms of YHWH worship. The combination of sacred stones and Asherah poles on 'every high hill and under every green tree' is the standard Deuteronomistic description of full-spectrum Canaanite syncretism.
11: 'They burned offerings at all the high places as the nations had done' (wayhaqṭᵉrû šām bᵉḵol-habbāmôt kaggôyim ʾăšer heglāh YHWH mippᵉnêhem) returns to the governing irony: Israel's behavior mirrors the expelled nations. The phrase wayyaʿᵃśû dᵉḇārîm rāʿîm lᵉḵaʿᵃs ʾet-YHWH, 'they did evil things to provoke the LORD to anger,' introduces the kaʿas (provocation) theme that runs through the Deuteronomistic assessment of the kings; YHWH's response to kaʿas is the withdrawal of protection and the activation of covenant curses.
12: Israel 'served idols' (wayyaʿaḇdû haggillûlîm) — the word gillûlîm (used 38 times in Ezekiel and here) is a highly polemical term, often understood as a derivation meaning 'dung-pellets' or 'dung-balls,' a deliberate scatological coinage to disparage idols. The explicit divine prohibition — ʾăšer ʾāmar lahem YHWH lōʾ taʿᵃśûn ʾet-haddāḇār hazzeh, 'despite the LORD saying to them: do not do this thing' — frames the idolatry as willful defiance of a clear command rather than ignorance. The Deuteronomistic framework is that Israel had full covenantal knowledge (Deuteronomy 4:15-20 explicitly warns against the gods represented by the host of heaven and carved images) and violated it deliberately.
13: YHWH's prophetic warnings — sent 'through every prophet and seer' (bᵉyaḏ kol nāḇîʾ kol ḥōzeh) — form the second half of the theological indictment: not only was Israel warned by the written law, but YHWH sent continuous prophetic messengers. The specific content of the warning is given: šûḇû middarᵉḵêḵem hārāʿîm wᵉšimrû miṣwōtay ḥuqqôtay kᵉḵol-hattôrāh ʾăšer ṣiwwîtî ʾet ʾăḇōtêḵem wᵉkāʾăšer šālaḥtî ʾalêḵem bᵉyaḏ ʿᵃḇāḏay hanᵉḇîʾîm, 'turn from your evil ways and keep my commandments and statutes — all the law I gave your ancestors, which I sent to you through my servant the prophets.' The prophets are the living voice of the written Torah; their cumulative rejection across 200 years of the northern kingdom is the measure of the apostasy's depth.
14: Israel 'would not listen but hardened their neck like their ancestors who had not trusted in the LORD their God' (wᵉlōʾ šāmēʿû wayyaqšû ʾet ʿārpām kᵉʿōrep ʾăḇōtām ʾăšer lōʾ heʾěmînû baYHWH ʾelōhêhem). The 'stiff neck' image (qᵉšēh ʿōrep) is first used in Exodus 32:9 to describe Israel at the golden-calf incident; the Deuteronomist draws an explicit line from the wilderness apostasy to the northern kingdom's exile, framing the entire history as a single continuous failure. The contrast with faith (heʾěmîn — trusted, believed) is significant: the prophets called for covenant trust; Israel chose autonomy.
15: Israel 'rejected his statutes and the covenant he had made with their ancestors, and the warnings he had given them; they pursued worthless idols and became worthless themselves' (wayyimᵉʾăsû ʾet ḥuqqāyw wᵉʾet bᵉrîtô ʾăšer kārat ʾet ʾăḇôtām wᵉʾēt ʿēḏwōtāyw ʾăšer hēʿîḏ bām wayyēlᵉḵû ʾaḥᵃrê hahaḇel wayyehbālû). The wordplay on heḇel (vanity, vapor, worthlessness) — 'they pursued hebel and became hebel' — is one of Kings' most pointed theological formulations. Idols are heḇel (the word Ecclesiastes will use for all transient things; the name Abel/Hebel in Gen 4 may carry the same resonance of brevity). Israel became what it worshipped. The surrounding nations' practices that YHWH had forbidden to Israel are the standard of comparison: Israel's holiness was supposed to be the distinction; apostasy erased it.
16: The full apostasy catalogue: abandoned all YHWH's commandments → cast two metal calves (ʿēgālîm massēḵāh šᵉnayim, Jeroboam's calves of Bethel and Dan, 1 Kings 12:28) → made an Asherah pole → worshipped the whole host of heaven (kol ṣᵉḇāʾ haššāmayim, astral worship including sun, moon, stars, and the divine council in astral form) → served Baal. The calf worship, Asherah, and astral worship are all attested in the Iron Age archaeological record from Israelite sites: Megiddo, Hazor, Beth-shan, and Tell el-Far'ah North all show evidence of non-YHWH cult objects alongside Israelite material culture. The catalogue is structured to show comprehensive abandonment: no commandment or covenant feature is left untouched.
17: The final items in the apostasy catalogue — burning children in the fire, practicing divination (qāsam qᵉsāmîm) and sorcery (nāḥaš niḥûšîm), and selling themselves completely to evil (wayyiḥtamkᵉrû lāʿăśôt hārāʿ) — represent the most severe Deuteronomic prohibitions. Child sacrifice is the capital offense of Leviticus 20:2; divination and sorcery are condemned in Deuteronomy 18:10-12 as the defining practices of the nations YHWH expelled; the phrase 'sold themselves to do evil' echoes 1 Kings 21:20 (Ahab 'sold himself to do evil') — the voluntary, commercial metaphor for sin as self-enslavement. The comprehensive nature of the apostasy list justifies the comprehensive nature of the punishment.
18: 'So the LORD was extremely angry with Israel and expelled them from his sight' (wayyitʾannap YHWH mᵉʾōḏ bᵉYiśrāʾēl wayyᵉsîrēm mēʿal pānāyw lōʾ nišʾar raq šēḇeṭ yᵉhûḏāh lᵉḇaddô). The verb ʾānap (be angry) with the intensifier mᵉʾōḏ (exceedingly) marks the divine anger at its highest register in the narrative. 'Expelled from his sight' (wayyᵉsîrēm mēʿal pānāyw) is the ultimate covenant curse — the loss of divine presence, the reversal of the central covenant promise (Exodus 33:14: 'My presence will go with you'). The note that 'only the tribe of Judah remained' is simultaneously a word of partial grace — the Davidic covenant still holds one tribe — and an anticipation of 2 Kings 25, where Judah will follow the same trajectory to the same judgment 136 years later.
19: The addendum — 'Judah also did not obey the commands of the LORD their God but followed the practices that Israel had introduced' (gam Yᵉhûḏāh lōʾ šāmar ʾet miṣwōt YHWH ʾelōhêhem wayyēlᵉḵû bᵉḥuqqôt Yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer ʿāśû) — is a theological footnote that prevents the reader from taking comfort in Judah's survival. Israel pioneered the apostasy; Judah followed. The exile of the north is not just Israel's story but a warning about Judah's trajectory. The Kings account will trace Judah's subsequent spiral through Manasseh's extreme apostasy (2 Kings 21) to Josiah's reform (2 Kings 22-23) to the final Babylonian deportations (2 Kings 24-25).
20: 'The LORD rejected all the descendants of Israel, afflicted them, and handed them over to raiders, until he had expelled them from his sight' (wayyimʾas YHWH bᵉḵol zeraʿ Yiśrāʾēl wayyᵉʿannēm wayyittᵉnēm bᵉyaḏ šōsîm ʿaḏ ʾăšer hišlîḵām mēʿal pānāyw). The verb māʾas (reject, spurn) is used by Hosea (Hosea 4:6: 'since you have rejected knowledge, I will reject you from being my priest') and Amos (Amos 2:4: 'because they rejected the law of the LORD') — the prophetic interpretation and the Deuteronomistic explanation use the same vocabulary. The 'raiders' who afflicted Israel during the period of the judges through the kings are reframed as YHWH's instruments of progressive punishment, building toward the final expulsion.
21: The narrative traces the apostasy back to its origin point: 'when Israel was torn away from the house of David, they made Jeroboam son of Nebat king, and Jeroboam led Israel away from following the LORD and caused them to commit great sin' (kî qāraʿ Yiśrāʾēl mēʿal bêt Dāwîḏ wayyamlîḵû ʾet Yarāḇᵉʿām ben-Nᵉḇāṭ... wayyaddaḥ Yarāḇᵉʿām ʾet Yiśrāʾēl mēʾaḥᵃrê YHWH waheḥĕṭîʾām ḥaṭṭāʾāh gᵉḏôlāh). The Schism (1 Kgs 12) is identified as the moment when the trajectory toward exile became inevitable: not because it was geopolitically destabilizing, but because Jeroboam's substitute cult (the golden calves) permanently redirected northern worship away from Jerusalem and toward an idolatrous alternative. The 210 years from Jeroboam to Hoshea are a single long act of national apostasy rooted in one founding sin.
22: 'The Israelites continued in all the sins Jeroboam had committed; they never turned away from them' (wayyēlᵉḵû bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl bᵉḵol ḥaṭṭāʾôt Yarāḇᵉʿām ʾăšer ʿāśāh lōʾ sārû mimmennāh) — the climax of the theological explanation's backward reach. The entire history of the northern kings, with their individual variations in wickedness (some worse than others, but none departing from Jeroboam's core sin), is summarized in a single unbroken trajectory. The phrase lōʾ sārû mimmennāh (they did not turn from it) is the negative of the repentance (šûḇ) the prophets consistently called for.
23: 'Until the LORD removed Israel from his sight, just as he had declared through all his servants the prophets. So Israel was carried into exile from their land to Assyria — where they remain to this day' (ʿad ʾăšer-hēsîr YHWH ʾet Yiśrāʾēl mēʿal pānāyw kaʾăšer dibber bᵉyaḏ kol ʿăḇāḏāyw hanᵉḇîʾîm wayyigl Yiśrāʾēl mēʿal ʾadmātô ʾaššûr ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh). The phrase 'to this day' (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh) is a historiographic marker indicating the exile was recent and living memory for the text's audience. The Deuteronomistic History was compiled/finalized during or shortly after the Babylonian exile; the northern exile had been a reality for over a century. 'As he declared through all his servants the prophets' ties the historical event to the prophetic corpus — the exile validates the prophets rather than refuting them.
24: The Assyrian resettlement policy — bringing populations from Babylon, Cuthah, Avva, Hamath, and Sepharvaim to replace the deported Israelites — is part of Sargon II's documented imperial strategy of population interchange. Sargon's annals state: 'the people of the lands conquered by my hands... I settled there; Assyrian administrators I set over them.' The imported populations came from multiple Mesopotamian and Syrian regions, creating a mixed society in the former northern kingdom. The later Samaritan identity grows from this mixed population — the returning exiles under Ezra and Nehemiah viewed Samaritans as disqualified from temple participation (Ezra 4:1-3) precisely because their ancestry was not purely Israelite. The NT Samaritan context (John 4, Luke 10:25-37) inherits this historical and ethnic complexity.
25: The settler populations' ignorance of 'the requirements of the god of the land' (mišpaṭ ʾelōhê hāʾāreṣ) triggers a lion attack, which YHWH sends. The ANE background is territorial-deity theology (see the Naaman narrative, 2 Kings 5:17): every land had its god, whose requirements had to be met to avoid divine punishment. The lions are a covenant curse from Leviticus 26:22 ('I will send wild beasts among you which shall rob you of your children and destroy your cattle and make you few in number') — though here applied to settlers rather than Israel. The dynamic is that even the pagan settlers recognize the principle of territorial divine management, while Israel (the people who should have known YHWH's requirements) failed to follow them.
26: The report to the Assyrian king — that the settler peoples don't know the mišpaṭ ʾelōhê hāʾāreṣ (requirements of the land's god) — uses precisely the territorial-deity framework that Naaman operated within (2 Kings 5:17). The irony is multilayered: non-Israelite settlers recognize that YHWH controls this land and that his requirements must be met; the Israelites who knew his covenant requirements never properly met them. The king of Assyria's practical response (send a priest to teach them) treats YHWH as one of many territorial deities to be managed — a thoroughly ANE-pagan interpretation of the God whose covenant with Israel Israel itself had treated with equal casualness.
27: The Assyrian king's command — send back one of the exiled priests to live in Bethel and teach the people 'the requirements of the god of the land' — is historically plausible as Assyrian administrative management: deportation policy was pragmatic, and a resident population required religious stability for political management. The specific instruction to send the priest to Bethel is pointed: Bethel was the site of Jeroboam's golden-calf sanctuary (1 Kings 12:29), the epicenter of the northern kingdom's original apostasy. The priest who teaches YHWH worship there is inheriting a fundamentally compromised cult site.
28: The returning Israelite priest settles in Bethel and teaches the people 'how to worship the LORD' (wayyᵉhi mᵉlammēḏ ʾōtām ʾêḵ yîrᵉʾû ʾet YHWH). The verb yārēʾ (fear, revere) is the standard covenant-fear vocabulary; the priest presumably teaches some form of YHWH worship. However, the content of his teaching is compromised by two factors: (1) the Bethel location was associated with the calf-worship that the Deuteronomist considered fundamental apostasy; (2) the settlers continue their own deity practices alongside (vv29-33). The teaching produces syncretism, not conversion.
29: The imported nations manufacture their own gods — wayhî maʿᵃśēh kol gôy wᵉgôy ʾelōhêhem, 'each nation made its gods' — and install them in the high-place shrines (bêt habbāmôt) the Samaritans had built. The high-place infrastructure that the Israelites had built for syncretic YHWH worship is now repurposed for purely pagan worship by the immigrant populations. Each settler community imports its own national deity into the Israelite sacred geography, creating a religious patchwork that reverses the Conquest's purification of the land's cult sites.
30: The Babylonians made Succoth-benoth (Sukkôt bᵉnôt — possibly 'the booths of the daughters,' a name that may relate to a Babylonian astral deity, possibly Zarpanitum, consort of Marduk); the Cutheans made Nergal (the Babylonian underworld deity, god of pestilence and the dead, worshipped at Cutha/Tell Ibrahim in modern Iraq); the Hamathites made Ashima (ʾăšîmāʾ — a deity attested in Aramaic inscriptions from Hamath, possibly a form of Ishtar or a local deity). These deities are largely confirmed in cuneiform and Aramaic sources; the biblical naming preserves historical memory of the specific cult communities that settled in the former northern territory.
31: The Avvites made Nibhaz (niḇḥaz — possibly a dog-deity, corresponding to the Elamite deity Ibnahaza or a corruption of Anubis) and Tartak (unidentified; possibly an Aramaean deity). The Sepharvites burned their children to Adrammelech (ʾaḏrammeleḵ — possibly a form of the Mesopotamian deity Adad combined with melek, 'king') and Anammelech (ʿanammeleḵ — possibly Anu + melek, the Mesopotamian sky god). The child sacrifice practices of the Sepharvites mirror Ahaz's child sacrifice to Molech (2 Kings 16:3), creating an ironic parallel: the sin that characterized Judah's worst king is now practiced by pagan settlers in the former northern territory. The land that could not tolerate Israel's sins is now occupied by peoples practicing the same or worse sins.
32: The settlers 'professed to fear the LORD' (wayhû yᵉrēʾîm ʾet YHWH) while appointing their own priests for the high places. The appointment of non-Levitical, ethnically mixed priests for the high-place shrines parallels Jeroboam's appointment of non-Levitical priests (1 Kings 12:31, 2 Kings 13:33); both represent the principle that the cult can be managed by whoever is politically convenient rather than whoever is covenantally designated. The fear vocabulary here is the language of pagan territorial-deity management, not the covenant-fear the law demands.
33: 'They worshiped the LORD, but they also served their own gods in the way of the nations from which they had come' (ʾet YHWH hāyû yᵉrēʾîm wᵉʾet ʾelōhêhem hāyû ʿōḇdîm kᵉmišpaṭ haggôyim ʾăšer heglû ʾōtām miššām). This is the defining description of Samaritan religion: syncretistic fear of YHWH combined with ethnic-deity worship. The phrase 'in the way of the nations' (kᵉmišpaṭ haggôyim) uses the same mišpaṭ (requirement, custom, legal standard) that the settlers initially lacked (v25-26): they now have a mišpaṭ for YHWH alongside their ethnic mišpaṭ. The result is a syncretistic religion indistinguishable from the Canaanite cult the Conquest was meant to remove.
34: 'To this day they follow their original practices. They do not truly fear the LORD or observe the statutes, ordinances, laws, and commandments he gave to the descendants of Jacob, whom he named Israel' (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh hēm ʿōśîm kammišpāṭîm hārišōnîm ʾênām yᵉrēʾîm ʾet YHWH wᵉʾênām ʿōśîm kᵉḥuqqōtām ûkᵉmišpāṭêhem ûḵattôrāh ûḵammmiṣwāh ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH ʾet bᵉnê yaʿᵃqōḇ ʾăšer śām šᵉmô Yiśrāʾēl). The use of the name Israel — 'descendants of Jacob whom he named Israel' — asserts that the name Israel belongs properly to the covenant community descended from Jacob, not to the mixed settler population of Samaria. This naming-assertion anticipates the post-exilic controversy over who constitutes true Israel (Ezra 4, Neh 13).
35: YHWH's covenant command (vv35-39) is cited in full as the standard the settlers are failing to meet. The prohibition — 'you must not worship other gods or bow down to them or serve them or sacrifice to them' (lōʾ tîrᵉʾû ʾelōhîm ʾăḥērîm wᵉlōʾ tišᵉtaḥᵃwû lāhem wᵉlōʾ taʿᵃḇdûm wᵉlōʾ tizbᵉḥû lāhem) — restates the first and second commandments of the Decalogue (Exodus 20:3-5). The fourfold prohibition (fear, bow down, serve, sacrifice) covers the full range of cultic acts; the Samaritan settlers' practices (vv32-33) violate all four. The covenant is cited not to condemn the settlers (they were not party to it) but to define what true fear of YHWH looks like, in contrast to their syncretistic practice.
36: 'Rather, fear the LORD who brought you out of Egypt with great power and an outstretched arm' (kî ʾim ʾet YHWH ʾăšer heʿᵃlāh ʾetḵem mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim bᵉḵōaḥ gāḏôl ûḇizrôaʿ nᵉṭûyāh ʾōtô tîrᵉʾû wᵉlô tišᵉtaḥᵃwû wᵉlô tizbᵉḥû). The Exodus formulation is the defining ground of Israelite covenant obligation: YHWH's election-act creates the exclusive covenant claim. The 'outstretched arm' (zᵉrôaʿ nᵉṭûyāh) is standard Exodus liturgical language (Exodus 6:6, Deuteronomy 4:34, 2 Kings 5:15, 2 Kings 7:19). The settlers who had no part in the Exodus cannot respond to this appeal from within; the text's citation of it functions more as an indictment of Israel's failure to respond than as a command to the settlers.
37: The comprehensive command — observe forever the statutes, ordinances, laws, and commandments YHWH wrote for his people — uses four distinct legal terms: ḥuqqôt (statutes), mišpāṭîm (ordinances/judgments), tôrāh (law/instruction), miṣwāh (commandment). This legal quadruple also appears in 2 Chr 19:10 and reflects the Deuteronomistic understanding that the Torah has a comprehensive character: not just commandments or just statutes, but the entire integrated legal-covenantal deposit. The command to observe them 'forever' (kol hayyāmîm, all the days) excludes the possibility of situational non-compliance.
38: 'Do not forget the covenant I have made with you' (wᵉhabbᵉrît ʾăšer kāratî ʾittᵉḵem lōʾ tišᵉkāḥû) is the central anti-forgetting command of Deuteronomic theology. Deuteronomy 4:9, 2 Kings 6:12, and 8:11 repeatedly command Israel not to forget YHWH and his covenant; the prophets repeatedly charge Israel with forgetting (Hosea 8:14, 2 Kings 13:6). The history of the northern kingdom is precisely the history of covenantal forgetting: the Torah was not read publicly, the Passover was not kept (until Josiah, 2 Kings 23:22), and the covenant obligations were treated as optional rather than constitutive of national identity.
39: 'Fear only the LORD your God, and he will rescue you from all your enemies' (kî ʾim ʾet YHWH ʾelōhêḵem tîrᵉʾû wᵉhûʾ yaṣṣîl ʾetḵem mikkol ʾōyᵉḇêḵem) is the covenant's positive promise: monolatrous fear of YHWH yields military protection. The irony is that Israel sought protection from Assyria through tribute (Menahem), through coalition politics (Pekah), and through Egyptian alliance (Hoshea) — every avenue except covenant fidelity. The verse anticipates Hezekiah's vindication (2 Kings 18-19), where singular fear of YHWH (rather than Assyrian tribute or Egyptian alliance) results in miraculous deliverance from Sennacherib.
40: 'But they would not listen; they kept to their old ways' (wᵉlōʾ šāmēʿû kî ʾim kᵉmišpāṭām hārîʾšôn hēm ʿōśîm). The verse closes the covenant-exposition section with the same stubborn non-response that the text attributed to Israel in v14: wᵉlōʾ šāmēʿû (they did not listen). The settlers who replaced the Israelites exhibit the same fundamental orientation — following original practices rather than YHWH's covenant — that characterized the Israelites throughout the northern kingdom's history. The replacements are not an improvement; the land continues under the same spiritual pattern.
41: 'So these peoples worshiped the LORD but also served their own idols. Their children and grandchildren do the same today, just as their ancestors did' (wayhû haggôyim hāʾēlleh yᵉrēʾîm ʾet YHWH wᵉʾet pᵉsilêhem hāyû ʿōḇdîm gam bᵉnêhem ûḇnê bᵉnêhem kaʾăšer ʿāśû ʾăḇōtām hēm ʿōśîm ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh). The closing verse of 2 Kings 17 is simultaneously a historical statement about the Samaritan settlers and an anticipation of the Samaritan-Jewish controversy that runs from Ezra through the NT. The 'to this day' again connects the narrative to the author's present. The Samaritan woman at the well (John 4:20: 'Our fathers worshipped on this mountain, but you say Jerusalem is the place') and the healed Samaritan's gratitude (Luke 17:16) both operate against the background of this centuries-old religious ambiguity that v41 captures: part-worship of YHWH, part-worship of other gods, established as the inherited pattern of the land.
